text: Humans were living in hunter-gatherer bands — no writing, no cities, no agriculture. Everything made was made by hand from stone, bone, and wood.
The first cities and writing systems were emerging in Mesopotamia and Egypt. Humanity was inventing civilisation from scratch.
The Bronze Age was at its height. Great palace civilisations ruled the Mediterranean, and trade networks stretched from Britain to Babylon.
The Bronze Age had just collapsed in a matter of decades — whole empires vanished, trade networks broke down, and literacy nearly disappeared from the Mediterranean.
The Greek city-states were rising. Philosophy was being invented. The Persian Empire was the largest the world had ever seen.
The golden age of Athens — Socrates, Plato, the Parthenon. The world's first democracy was a radical experiment on a small peninsula.
Alexander the Great had spread Greek culture from Egypt to India. The Hellenistic world was a cosmopolitan melting pot of languages, religions, and ideas.
Rome was rising from Italian city-state to Mediterranean empire, fighting Carthage in the Punic Wars for control of the western world.
Rome was tearing itself apart in civil wars — Julius Caesar, Augustus, and the end of the Republic. The Mediterranean world was being unified under one ruler.
The Roman Empire was near its peak, stretching from Scotland to Mesopotamia. Over a quarter of humanity lived under Roman law. Roads and aqueducts connected the known world.
Christianity was spreading quietly through the Roman Empire, moving from a persecuted sect in Judea to a religion with followers from Spain to Persia.
The Roman Empire was fracturing — political instability, plague, and pressure from migrations were weakening the structure that had held the Mediterranean world together.
The Roman Empire was in its final decades. Germanic peoples were crossing the Rhine and Danube in waves. The world it had built was unravelling.
The Roman world had collapsed in the West. Cities were emptying, roads were unmaintained, and literacy was becoming rare. The Dark Ages had begun.
Islam had just been founded and was spreading with astonishing speed — within a century of Muhammad's death, Muslim armies reached Spain, Central Asia, and the edge of India.
Europe was fragmented into small, constantly warring kingdoms. The Islamic world was the most advanced civilisation on Earth, preserving and expanding the knowledge of antiquity.
The Vikings were raiding and trading from Newfoundland to Constantinople. Their longships could cross oceans or navigate the rivers deep into Russia.
Charlemagne had briefly united much of Western Europe — the first time since Rome. His empire was already fracturing among his grandsons after his death.
Medieval Europe was slowly recovering. The first universities were forming, trade was reviving, and the population was beginning to grow again after centuries of stagnation.
The Crusades were ongoing — European armies were fighting, trading, and dying in the Holy Land. It was an age of extraordinary violence and cultural contact between Christianity and Islam.
The Mongol Empire under Genghis Khan and his successors was the largest contiguous land empire in history, reshaping Eurasia from China to Poland in a generation.
Medieval Europe was at its demographic peak. Cathedrals were soaring above growing cities. No one knew what was coming.
Famines and extreme weather had been weakening Europe for years. Then the Black Death arrived from the East.
The Black Death was devastating Europe. One in three people would die — entire villages were wiped out, and the medieval world would never look the same again.
Europe was slowly recovering from the Black Death. The massive labour shortage gave surviving peasants new leverage — serfdom was weakening, wages were rising.
The Renaissance was flourishing in Italy. Art, science, and humanism were being rediscovered. Wealthy merchants in Florence were funding a cultural explosion.
The printing press had just been invented. For the first time, books could be mass-produced. Ideas that once took decades to spread now moved in years.
Constantinople had fallen to the Ottomans, ending the Byzantine Empire after a thousand years. The Ottoman Empire now dominated the eastern Mediterranean.
Europe had just 'discovered' the Americas. Millions of indigenous people were dying from diseases they had never encountered. Gold and silver were flowing east.
The potato, tomato, and maize had arrived in Europe from the Americas — but most Europeans had never tasted them and barely knew they existed.
The Reformation had shattered Christian unity in Europe. Catholics and Protestants were fighting bitter wars over which version of Christianity was true.
The potato was beginning to spread through Europe, but remained exotic and distrusted. It would take another century before it became a staple food for ordinary people.
The Scientific Revolution was underway — Galileo, Kepler, and Newton were rewriting humanity's understanding of the cosmos. The universe was larger and more mechanical than anyone had imagined.
The Thirty Years' War was devastating Central Europe — up to a third of the population of German-speaking lands would die before the Peace of Westphalia ended it.
Europe was emerging from a century of religious war, exhausted. Coffee houses were opening in every city, and the Enlightenment was just beginning to stir.
The Age of Reason was transforming European thought. Coffee houses were the social media of their day — where ideas, newspapers, and revolutions were all born.
The Enlightenment was at its peak. Voltaire, Rousseau, and Hume were questioning everything — religion, monarchy, and the nature of humanity itself.
The Industrial Revolution was underway in Britain — steam engines were replacing muscle power for the first time in human history. The world was about to change forever.
The American Revolution had created a republic based on Enlightenment ideals, and the French Revolution was reshaping Europe in blood and fire. The age of monarchy was ending.
Napoleon was at the peak of his power. His armies had remade the map of Europe, and his legal codes were reshaping how nations governed themselves.
After Napoleon's defeat, Europe's old monarchies were trying to restore the old order. Nationalism and liberalism were building underground, waiting to erupt.
Industrialisation was spreading from Britain to the Continent. Factories and slums were growing side by side. Charles Dickens was describing what he saw.
The revolutionary year of 1848 had shaken every throne in Europe. The new industrial working class was finding its voice — Karl Marx had just published the Communist Manifesto.
Railways were shrinking the world. A journey that took weeks now took days. For the first time, ordinary people could travel far from where they were born.
The American Civil War was being fought over slavery and the future of the United States — the bloodiest war in American history, with over 600,000 dead.
The Belle Époque — an era of optimism, technological wonder, and imperial expansion. Few suspected the catastrophe that was quietly being prepared.
Electricity was transforming city life — electric lights, trams, and the first telephones. Rural areas and most of the world saw almost none of it.
The world was more connected than ever before — and more heavily armed. Europe's great powers were locked in alliances that would drag them all into catastrophe.
The First World War was raging. An entire generation of young men was being killed in the trenches. It was the bloodiest war in history up to that point.
The Russian Revolution was reshaping the world. The Tsar had been overthrown, and the Soviet Union was being born from the chaos of civil war.
The war was over, but Europe was shattered. Empires had collapsed, the map was being redrawn, and the 1920s were roaring with jazz, anxiety, and borrowed money.
The Great Depression had collapsed the world economy. Millions were unemployed. Extreme politics — fascism and communism — were filling the vacuum left by failed democracy.
The Nazis were in power in Germany. Across Europe, democracies were falling and dictators rising. Most people still hoped a second world war could be avoided.
The Second World War was being fought across the globe. The Holocaust was systematically murdering millions. Over 70 million people would die before it ended.
The war had just ended. Europe lay in ruins. The atomic bomb had changed everything. The world was already dividing into two hostile blocs.
The Korean War was being fought — the first hot war of the Cold War era, with American and Chinese forces clashing directly on the peninsula.
Television was spreading into homes across the Western world for the first time — in black and white. It was fundamentally changing how people understood the world.
The Cold War was at its height. The US and Soviet Union were locked in an arms race and a space race, each genuinely convinced the other might strike first.
The Space Age had arrived — humans were going to the Moon. At the same time, the Civil Rights movement was reshaping American society, and the old order was being challenged everywhere.
The Vietnam War was consuming a generation. Protests were erupting across the Western world against the war, against racism, and against the established order.
Colour television was just beginning to spread into homes. The 1950s were still in living memory. A man had walked on the Moon.
The optimism of the 1960s had curdled. The oil crisis had ended the long post-war boom. Stagflation, political scandals, and terrorism were defining the decade.
The personal computer was arriving in homes. Most people had never heard of the internet. The Cold War was unexpectedly — and suddenly — ending.
The Soviet Union was collapsing from within. Gorbachev's reforms had unleashed forces he could not control. The Berlin Wall would soon fall.
The Berlin Wall had fallen. The Soviet Union was dissolving. For the first time in 45 years, the Cold War was over — and nobody quite knew what would come next.
The internet was spreading from universities to homes. A generation was going online for the first time. The world was becoming networked at a speed no one had predicted.
The World Wide Web was transforming everything. Most businesses had no website a decade earlier. E-mail was normal now. Google existed. The dot-com bubble had burst and rebuilt.
The September 11 attacks had launched a War on Terror. The world's attention shifted to Afghanistan and Iraq. Security and surveillance expanded everywhere.
Social media was being born — Facebook, YouTube, Twitter. Most people still used a camera separately from their phone. The smartphone was about to change everything.
The 2008 financial crisis had shaken the global economy. Banks had collapsed, millions had lost their homes, and governments were imposing austerity on their populations.
The smartphone had put a supercomputer in everyone's pocket. Social media was reshaping politics, attention, and the very nature of what people agreed was true.
Populism was rising across the democratic world — Brexit, Trump, and an era of profound political polarisation. Social media was accelerating the breakdown of shared reality.
The COVID-19 pandemic had shut down the world. Billions were in lockdown. It was the largest disruption to daily life since the Second World War.
Russia had invaded Ukraine, ending the post-Cold War peace in Europe. AI was beginning to transform work and creativity at a pace that made the future genuinely hard to predict.
This year lies in the future. The 22nd century is still unwritten — shaped by the choices made in the decades before it. Climate, technology, and civilization are at a crossroads.
This year lies far in the future. Whatever world exists in the 23rd century will be a reflection of the long chain of decisions, discoveries, and struggles that came before it.
This year is centuries ahead — so far into the future it is almost impossible to imagine. The people living then will look back on our era the way we look at medieval times.
